From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

For broader coverage of this topic, see [Comparison of American and British English](/wiki/Comparison_of_American_and_British_English "Comparison of American and British English").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Defence_Defense_Labour_Labor_British_American_spelling_by_country.svg/500px-Defence_Defense_Labour_Labor_British_American_spelling_by_country.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Defence_Defense_Labour_Labor_British_American_spelling_by_country.svg)

British and American spellings around the world:  

British *analyse/crystallise* (*crystallize* in [Oxford spelling](/wiki/Oxford_spelling "Oxford spelling"))*/centre/defence/labour/programme* (except for *computer program*) dominant; English is an official or majority language

American *analyze/crystallize* (less commonly *crystalize*)*/center* (except for some place names, e.g. *[Centre County](/wiki/Centre_County,_Pennsylvania "Centre County, Pennsylvania")*)*/defense/labor/program* dominant; English is an official or majority language

Canadian *analyze/crystallize/centre/defence/labour/program* dominant; English is one of two official languages along with French

Australian *analyse/crystallise/centre/defence/labour* (except for *[Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party")*)*/program* dominant; English is an official or majority language

English is not an official or majority language; British spelling is dominant

English is not an official or majority language; American spelling is dominant

English is not an official or majority language; inconsistent use of American and British spelling

Despite the various [English dialects](/wiki/List_of_dialects_of_English "List of dialects of English") spoken from country to country and within different regions of the same country, there are only slight regional variations in [English orthography](/wiki/English_orthography "English orthography"), the two most notable variations being British and American spelling. Many of [the differences](/wiki/Comparison_of_American_and_British_English "Comparison of American and British English") between [American](/wiki/American_English "American English") and [British](/wiki/British_English "British English") or [Commonwealth](/wiki/English_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations "English in the Commonwealth of Nations") English date back to a time before [spelling](/wiki/Spelling "Spelling") standards were developed. For instance, some spellings seen as "American" were once commonly used in the United Kingdom, and some spellings seen as "British" were once commonly used in the United States.

A "British standard" began to emerge following the 1755 publication of [Samuel Johnson](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson "Samuel Johnson")'s *[A Dictionary of the English Language](/wiki/A_Dictionary_of_the_English_Language "A Dictionary of the English Language")*, and an "American standard" started following the work of [Noah Webster](/wiki/Noah_Webster "Noah Webster") and, in particular, his *[An American Dictionary of the English Language](/wiki/Webster%27s_Dictionary "Webster's Dictionary")*, first published in 1828.[[1]](#cite_note-Micklethwait2005-1) Webster's efforts at [spelling reform](/wiki/Spelling_reform "Spelling reform") were effective in his native country, resulting in certain well-known patterns of spelling differences between the American and British varieties of English.

## Historical origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Historical origins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/British_spellings_in_NEJM_1814.jpg/250px-British_spellings_in_NEJM_1814.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_spellings_in_NEJM_1814.jpg)

An 1814 American medical text showing British English spellings that were still in use ("tumours", "colour", "centres", etc.)

In the early 18th century, [English spelling](/wiki/English_spelling "English spelling") was inconsistent. These differences became noticeable after the publication of influential [dictionaries](/wiki/Dictionary "Dictionary"). [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English") spellings mostly follow Johnson's *A Dictionary of the English Language* (1755), while many [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English") spellings follow Webster's *An American Dictionary of the English Language* ("ADEL", "Webster's Dictionary", 1828).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Webster was a proponent of [English spelling reform](/wiki/English-language_spelling_reform "English-language spelling reform") for reasons both [philological](/wiki/Philology "Philology") and nationalistic. In *A Companion to the American Revolution* (2008), [John Algeo](/wiki/John_Algeo "John Algeo") notes:

> it is often assumed that characteristically American spellings were invented by Noah Webster. He was very influential in popularizing certain spellings in the United States, but he did not originate them. Rather [...] he chose already existing options such as *center, color* and *check* for the simplicity, analogy or etymology.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s [first folios](/wiki/First_folio "First folio"), for example, used spellings such as *center* and *color* as much as *centre* and *colour*.[[4]](#cite_note-etymology_or-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Venezky1999-5) Webster did attempt to introduce some reformed spellings, as did the [Simplified Spelling Board](/wiki/Simplified_Spelling_Board "Simplified Spelling Board") in the early 20th century, but most were not adopted. In the United Kingdom, the influence of those who preferred the [Norman](/wiki/Norman_language "Norman language") (or [Anglo-French](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_language "Anglo-Norman language")) spellings of words proved to be decisive.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Later spelling adjustments in the United Kingdom had little effect on American spellings and vice versa.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Webster_Orthography_1828_%284-14%29.png/120px-Webster_Orthography_1828_%284-14%29.png)](/wiki/File:Webster_Orthography_1828_(4-14).png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Webster_Orthography_1828_%2815%29.png/120px-Webster_Orthography_1828_%2815%29.png)](/wiki/File:Webster_Orthography_1828_(15).png)

Extract from the Orthography section of the first edition (1828) of [Webster](/wiki/Noah_Webster "Noah Webster")'s "[ADEL](/wiki/Webster%27s_Dictionary "Webster's Dictionary")", which popularized the "American standard" spellings of *-er* (6); *-or* (7); the dropped *-e* (8); *-se* (11); and the doubling of consonants with a suffix (15).

For the most part, the spelling systems of most [Commonwealth countries](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and Ireland closely resemble the British system. In Canada, the spelling system can be said to follow both British and American forms,[[6]](#cite_note-Clark,_2009-6) and Canadians are somewhat more tolerant of foreign spellings than are other English-speaking nationalities.[[7]](#cite_note-Chambers1998-7) [Australian English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English") mostly follows British spelling norms but has strayed slightly, with some American spellings incorporated as standard.[[8]](#cite_note-Fourth_Edition_2005-8) [New Zealand English](/wiki/New_Zealand_English "New Zealand English") is almost identical to British spelling, except in the word *fiord* (instead of *fjord*). There is an increasing use of [macrons](/wiki/Macron_(diacritic) "Macron (diacritic)") in words that originated in [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language") and an unambiguous preference for *-ise* endings (see below).

## Latin-derived spellings (often through Romance)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Latin-derived spellings (often through Romance)")]

### *-our*, *-or*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: -our, -or")]

Most words ending in an unstressed *‑our* in British English (e.g., *[behaviour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/behaviour "wikt:behaviour")*, *[colour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/colour "wikt:colour")*, *[favour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/favour "wikt:favour")*,
*[flavour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/flavour "wikt:flavour")*, *[harbour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/harbour "wikt:harbour")*, *[honour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/honour "wikt:honour")*, *[humour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/humour "wikt:humour")*, *[labour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/labour "wikt:labour")*, *[neighbour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/neighbour "wikt:neighbour")*, *[rumour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/rumour "wikt:rumour")*, *[splendour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/splendour "wikt:splendour")*) end in *‑or* in American English (*[behavior](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/behavior "wikt:behavior")*, *[color](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/color "wikt:color")*, *[favor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/favor "wikt:favor")*,
*[flavor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/flavor "wikt:flavor")*, *[harbor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/harbor "wikt:harbor")*, *[honor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/honor "wikt:honor")*, *[humor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/humor "wikt:humor")*, *[labor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/labor "wikt:labor")*, *[neighbor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/neighbor "wikt:neighbor")*, *[rumor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/rumor "wikt:rumor")*, *[splendor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/splendor "wikt:splendor")*). Wherever the vowel is [unreduced in pronunciation](/wiki/Reduced_vowels_in_English "Reduced vowels in English") (e.g., *[devour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/devour "wikt:devour")*, *[contour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/contour "wikt:contour")*, *[flour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/flour "wikt:flour")*, *[hour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/hour "wikt:hour")*, *[paramour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/paramour "wikt:paramour")*, *[tour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/tour "wikt:tour")*, *[troubadour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/troubadour "wikt:troubadour")*, and *[velour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/velour "wikt:velour")*), the spelling is uniform everywhere.

Most words of this kind came from Latin, where the ending was spelled *‑or*. They were first adopted into English from early [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French"), and the ending was spelled *‑our*, *‑or* or *‑ur*.[[9]](#cite_note-Webster's_Third,_p._24a-9) After the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"), the ending became *‑our* to match the later Old French spelling.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The *‑our* ending was used not only in new English borrowings, but was also applied to the earlier borrowings that had used *‑or*.[[9]](#cite_note-Webster's_Third,_p._24a-9) However, *‑or* was still sometimes found.[[11]](#cite_note-SOED_1987-11) The first three folios of [Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s plays used both spellings before they were standardised to *‑our* in the Fourth Folio of 1685.[[4]](#cite_note-etymology_or-4)

After the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), new borrowings from Latin were taken up with their original *‑or* ending, and many words once ending in *‑our* (for example, *chancellour* and *governour*) reverted to *‑or*. A few words of the *‑our/or* group do not have a Latin counterpart that ends in *‑or*; for example, *armo(u)r*, *behavio(u)r*, *harbo(u)r*, *neighbo(u)r*; also *[arbo(u)r](/wiki/Pergola "Pergola")*, meaning "shelter", though senses "tree" and "tool" are always *[arbor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/arbor "wikt:arbor")*, a [false cognate](/wiki/False_cognate "False cognate") of the other word. The word *arbor* would be more accurately spelled *arber* or *arbre* in the US and the UK, respectively, the latter of which is the French word for "tree". Some 16th- and early 17th-century British scholars indeed insisted that *‑or* be used for words from Latin (e.g., *color*)[[11]](#cite_note-SOED_1987-11) and *‑our* for French loans; however, in many cases, the etymology was not clear, and therefore some scholars advocated *‑or* only and others *‑our* only.[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12)

Webster's 1828 dictionary had only *-or* and is given much of the credit for the adoption of this form in the United States. By contrast, Johnson's 1755 (pre-US independence and establishment) dictionary used *-our* for all words still so spelled in the United Kingdom (like *colour*), but also for words where the *u* has since been dropped: *ambassadour*, *emperour*, *errour*, *governour*, *horrour*, *inferiour*, *mirrour*, *perturbatour*, *superiour*, *tenour*, *terrour*, *tremour*. Johnson, unlike Webster, was not an advocate of spelling reform, but chose the spelling best derived, as he saw it, from among the variations in his sources. He preferred French over Latin spellings because, as he put it, "the French generally supplied us".[[13]](#cite_note-13) English speakers who moved to the United States took these preferences with them. In the early 20th century, [H. L. Mencken](/wiki/H._L._Mencken "H. L. Mencken") notes that "*honor* appears in the 1776 [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), but it seems to have been put there rather by accident than by design". In [Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson")'s original draft it is spelled "honour".[[14]](#cite_note-14) In the United Kingdom, examples of *[behavior](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/behavior "wikt:behavior")*, *[color](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/color "wikt:color")*, *[flavor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/flavor "wikt:flavor")*, *[harbor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/harbor "wikt:harbor")*, and *[neighbor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/neighbor "wikt:neighbor")* rarely appear in [Old Bailey](/wiki/Old_Bailey "Old Bailey") court records from the 17th and 18th centuries, whereas there are thousands of examples of their *-our* counterparts.[[15]](#cite_note-15) One notable exception is *honor*. *Honor* and *honour* were equally frequent in the United Kingdom until the 17th century;[[16]](#cite_note-16) *honor* only exists in the UK now as the spelling of *[Honor Oak](/wiki/Honor_Oak "Honor Oak")*, a district of London, and of the occasional given name [Honor](/wiki/Honor_(given_name) "Honor (given name)").

#### Derivatives and inflected forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Derivatives and inflected forms")]

In derivatives and [inflected](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") forms of the *-our/or* words, British usage depends on the nature of the [suffix](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix") used. The *u* is kept before English suffixes that are freely attachable to English words (for example in *[humourless](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/humourless "wikt:humourless")*, *[neighbourhood](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/neighbourhood "wikt:neighbourhood")*, and *[savoury](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/savoury "wikt:savoury")*) and suffixes of Greek or Latin origin that have been adopted into English (for example in *[behaviourism](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/behaviourism "wikt:behaviourism")*, *[favourite](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/favourite "wikt:favourite")*, and *[honourable](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/honourable "wikt:honourable")*). However, before Latin suffixes that are not freely attachable to English words, the *u*:

* may be dropped, for example in *[honorary](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/honorary "wikt:honorary")*, *[honorific](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/honorific "wikt:honorific")*, *[humorist](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/humorist "wikt:humorist")*, *[humorous](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/humorous "wikt:humorous")*, *[invigorate](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/invigorate "wikt:invigorate")*, *[laborious](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/laborious "wikt:laborious")*, and *[vigorous](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/vigorous "wikt:vigorous")*;
* may be either dropped or kept, for example in *colo(u)ration* and *colo(u)rize* or *colo(u)rise*; or
* may be kept, for example in *[colourist](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/colourist "wikt:colourist")*.[[9]](#cite_note-Webster's_Third,_p._24a-9)

In American usage, derivatives and inflected forms are built by simply adding the suffix in all cases (for example, *[favorite](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/favorite "wikt:favorite")*, *savory* etc.) since the *u* is absent to begin with.

#### Exceptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Exceptions")]

American usage, in most cases, keeps the *u* in the word *[glamour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/glamour "wikt:glamour")*, which comes from [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"), not Latin or French. *[Glamor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/glamor "wikt:glamor")* is sometimes used in imitation of the spelling reform of other *-our* words to *-or*. Nevertheless, the adjective *[glamorous](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/glamorous "wikt:glamorous")* often drops the first "u". *[Saviour](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/saviour "wikt:saviour")* is a somewhat common variant of *[savior](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/savior "wikt:savior")* in the US. The British spelling is very common for *honour* (and *favour*) in the formal language of [wedding invitations](/wiki/Wedding_invitation "Wedding invitation") in the US.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The name of the [Space Shuttle *Endeavour*](/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Endeavour "Space Shuttle Endeavour") has a *u* in it because the [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft") was named after British [Captain James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook")'s ship, [HMS *Endeavour*](/wiki/HMS_Endeavour "HMS Endeavour"). The (former) special car on [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")'s *[Coast Starlight](/wiki/Coast_Starlight "Coast Starlight")* train is known as the [Pacific Parlour](/wiki/Pacific_Parlour "Pacific Parlour") car, not *Pacific Parlor*. [Proper names](/wiki/Proper_name "Proper name") such as *[Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Pearl_Harbor "Pearl Harbor")* or *[Sydney Harbour](/wiki/Sydney_Harbour "Sydney Harbour")* are usually spelled according to their native-variety spelling vocabulary.

The name of the herb *[savory](/wiki/Satureja "Satureja")* is spelled thus everywhere, although the related adjective *savo(u)ry*, like *savo(u)r*, has a *u* in the UK. *Honor* (the name) and *arbor* (the tool) have *-or* in the United Kingdom, as mentioned above, as does the word *pallor*. As a general noun, *[rigour](/wiki/Rigour "Rigour")* [/ˈrɪɡər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") has a *u* in the UK; the medical term *[rigor](/wiki/Rigor_(medicine) "Rigor (medicine)")* (sometimes [/ˈraɪɡər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[18]](#cite_note-18) does not, such as in *rigor mortis*, which is Latin. Derivations of *rigour*/*rigor* such as *rigorous*, however, are typically spelled without a *u*, even in the UK. Words with the ending *-irior*, *-erior* or similar are spelled thus everywhere. *Junior* and *senior* were borrowed directly from Latin in the 13th century (as adjectives for father-son namesakes), and have never had *-our* forms anywhere.

The word *[armour](/wiki/Armour "Armour")* was once somewhat common in American usage but has disappeared except in some brand names such as [Under Armour](/wiki/Under_Armour "Under Armour").

The agent suffix *-or* (*separator*, *elevator*, *translator*, *animator*, etc.) is spelled thus both in American and British English.

#### Commonwealth usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Commonwealth usage")]

Commonwealth countries normally follow British usage. [Canadian English](/wiki/Canadian_English "Canadian English") most commonly uses the *-our* ending and *-our-* in derivatives and inflected forms. However, owing to the close geographic, historic, economic, and cultural relationship with the United States, *-or* endings are also sometimes used. Throughout the late 19th and early to mid-20th century, most Canadian newspapers chose to use the American usage of *-or* endings, originally to save time and money in the era of manual [movable type](/wiki/Movable_type "Movable type").[[19]](#cite_note-OC31Mar1990-19) However, in the 1990s, the majority of Canadian newspapers officially updated their spelling policies to the British usage of *-our*. This coincided with a renewed interest in Canadian English, and the release of the updated *[Gage Canadian Dictionary](/wiki/Gage_Canadian_Dictionary "Gage Canadian Dictionary")* in 1997 and the first *[Canadian Oxford Dictionary](/wiki/Canadian_Oxford_Dictionary "Canadian Oxford Dictionary")* in 1998. Historically, most libraries and educational institutions in Canada have supported the use of the *Oxford English Dictionary* rather than the American *Webster's Dictionary*. The use of a distinctive set of Canadian English spellings is viewed by many Canadians as one of the unique aspects of Canadian culture (especially when compared to the United States).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In Australia, *-or* endings enjoyed some use throughout the 19th century and in the early 20th century. Like Canada, though, most major Australian newspapers have switched from "*-or*" endings to "*-our*" endings. The "*-our*" spelling is taught in schools nationwide as part of the Australian curriculum. The most notable countrywide use of the *-or* ending is for one of the country's major political parties, the [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party"), which was originally called "the Australian Labour Party" (name adopted in 1908), but was frequently referred to as both "Labour" and "Labor". The "Labor" spelling was adopted from 1912 onward due to the influence of the [American labor movement](/wiki/American_labor_movement "American labor movement")[[20]](#cite_note-20) and [King O'Malley](/wiki/King_O%27Malley "King O'Malley"). On top of that, some place names in South Australia such as [Victor Harbor](/wiki/Victor_Harbor,_South_Australia "Victor Harbor, South Australia"), Franklin Harbor or Outer Harbor are usually spelled with the -or spellings. Aside from that, *-our* is now almost universal in Australia but the *-or* endings remain a minority variant. [New Zealand English](/wiki/New_Zealand_English "New Zealand English"), while sharing some words and syntax with [Australian English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English"), follows British usage.

### *-re*, *-er*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: -re, -er")]

In British English, some words from French, Latin or Greek end with a consonant followed by an unstressed *-re* (pronounced /ə(r)/). In modern American English, most of these words have the ending *-er*.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) The difference is most common for words ending in *-bre* or *-tre*: British spellings *[calibre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/calibre "wikt:calibre")*, *[centre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/centre "wikt:centre")*, *[fibre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/fibre "wikt:fibre")*, *[goitre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/goitre "wikt:goitre")*, *[litre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/litre "wikt:litre")*, *[lustre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/lustre "wikt:lustre")*, *[manoeuvre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/manoeuvre "wikt:manoeuvre")*, *[meagre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/meagre "wikt:meagre")*, *[metre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/metre "wikt:metre") (length)*, *[mitre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mitre "wikt:mitre")*, *[nitre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/nitre "wikt:nitre")*, *[ochre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/ochre "wikt:ochre")*, *[reconnoitre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/reconnoitre "wikt:reconnoitre")*, *[sabre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sabre "wikt:sabre")*, *[saltpetre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/saltpetre "wikt:saltpetre")*, *[sepulchre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sepulchre "wikt:sepulchre")*, *[sombre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sombre "wikt:sombre")*, *[spectre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/spectre "wikt:spectre")*, *[theatre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/theatre "wikt:theatre")* (see [exceptions](#Exceptions_2)) and *[titre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/titre "wikt:titre")* all have *-er* in American spelling.

In the United Kingdom, both *-re* and *-er* spellings were common before [Johnson's 1755 dictionary](/wiki/A_Dictionary_of_the_English_Language "A Dictionary of the English Language") was published. Following this, *-re* became the most common usage in the United Kingdom. In the United States, following the publication of *[Webster's Dictionary](/wiki/Webster%27s_Dictionary "Webster's Dictionary")* in the early 19th century, American English became more standardized, exclusively using the *-er* spelling.[[5]](#cite_note-Venezky1999-5)

In addition, the spelling of some words have been changed from *-re* to *-er* in both varieties. These include *amber*, *blister*, *cadaver*, *canister*, *[chamber](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/chamber "wikt:chamber")*, *[chapter](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/chapter "wikt:chapter")*, *charter*, *cider*, *coffer*, *coriander*, *cover*, *cucumber*, *cylinder*, *[December](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/December "wikt:December")*, *diaper*, *[disaster](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/disaster "wikt:disaster")*, *[enter](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/enter "wikt:enter")*, *fever*, *[filter](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/filter "wikt:filter")*, *gender*, *leper*, *[letter](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/letter "wikt:letter")*, *lobster*, *master*, *[member](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/member "wikt:member")*, *[meter](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/meter "wikt:meter") (measuring instrument)*, *[minister](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/minister "wikt:minister")*, *[monster](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/monster "wikt:monster")*, *murder*, *[November](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/November "wikt:November")*, *[number](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/number "wikt:number")*, *[October](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/October "wikt:October")*, *[offer](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/offer "wikt:offer")*, *order*, *[oyster](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/oyster "wikt:oyster")*, *[powder](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/powder "wikt:powder")*, *[proper](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/proper "wikt:proper")*, *render*, *[semester](/wiki/Semester "Semester")*, *[September](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/September "wikt:September")*, *sequester*, *sinister*, *[sober](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sober "wikt:sober")*, *surrender*, *[tender](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/tender "wikt:tender")*, and *[tiger](/wiki/Tiger "Tiger")*. Words using the *[-meter](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-meter "wikt:-meter")* suffix (from Ancient Greek -[μέτρον](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BC%CE%AD%CF%84%CF%81%CE%BF%CE%BD "wikt:μέτρον") *métron*, via French *[-mètre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-m%C3%A8tre "wikt:-mètre")*) normally had the *-re* spelling from earliest use in English but were superseded by *-er*. Examples include *[thermometer](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/thermometer "wikt:thermometer")* and *[barometer](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/barometer "wikt:barometer")*.

The *e* preceding the *r* is kept in American-inflected forms of nouns and verbs, for example, *fibers*, *reconnoitered*, *centering*, which are *fibres*, *reconnoitred*, and *centring* respectively in British English. According to the *OED*, *centring* is a *"word ... of 3 syllables (in careful pronunciation)"*[[23]](#cite_note-23) (i.e., /ˈsɛntərɪŋ/), yet there is no vowel in the spelling corresponding to the second syllable (/ə/). The OED third edition (revised entry of June 2016) allows either two or three syllables. On the [Oxford Dictionaries Online](/wiki/OED "OED") website, the three-syllable version is listed only as the American pronunciation of *centering*. The *e* is dropped for other derivations, for example, *central*, *fibrous*, *spectral*. However, the existence of related words without *e* before the *r* is not proof for the existence of an *-re* British spelling: for example, *entry* and *entrance* come from *enter*, which has not been spelled *entre* for centuries.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The difference relates only to root words; *-er* rather than *-re* is universal as a suffix for agentive (*reader*, *user*, *winner*) and comparative (*louder*, *nicer*) forms. One outcome is the British distinction of *[meter](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/meter "wikt:meter")* for a [measuring instrument](/wiki/Measuring_instrument "Measuring instrument") from *metre* for the unit of length. However, while "[poetic metre](/wiki/Metre_(poetry) "Metre (poetry)")" is often spelled as *-re*, [pentameter](/wiki/Iambic_pentameter "Iambic pentameter"), [hexameter](/wiki/Hexameter "Hexameter"), etc. are always *-er*.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

#### Exceptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Exceptions")]

Many other words have *-er* in British English. These include Germanic words, such as *anger*, *mother*, *timber* and *water*, and such Romance-derived words as *danger*, *quarter* and *river*.

The ending *-cre*, as in *acre*,[[26]](#cite_note-acre/louvre-26) *[lucre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/lucre "wikt:lucre")*, *[massacre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/massacre "wikt:massacre")*, and *[mediocre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mediocre "wikt:mediocre")*, is used in both British and American English to show that the *c* is pronounced /k/ rather than /s/. The spellings *euchre* and *ogre* are also the same in both British and American English.

*[Theater](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/theater "wikt:theater")* is the prevailing American spelling used to refer to both the dramatic arts and buildings where stage performances and screenings of films take place (i.e., "movie theaters").[[27]](#cite_note-27) National US newspapers such as *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* use *theater* in their entertainment sections, the *Times* even corrects proper names from *theatre* to *theater.*[[28]](#cite_note-WK15-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-ANT-30) The spelling *theatre* was previously more common, prevailing into at least the 1960s, with organizations such as [Theatre Communications Group](/wiki/Theatre_Communications_Group "Theatre Communications Group"), founded 1961, and [The Guthrie Theater](/wiki/The_Guthrie_Theater "The Guthrie Theater"), founded 1963, using it.[[28]](#cite_note-WK15-28) *The New York Times* switched to using *theater* in 1962,[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The Guthrie changed its spelling in 1971,[[33]](#cite_note-33) and the spelling has become increasingly common since.[[28]](#cite_note-WK15-28)

The spelling *[theatre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/theatre "wikt:theatre")* is a variant in American English.[[34]](#cite_note-34) It appears frequently in names, such as those of many New York City theatres on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre"),[[35]](#cite_note-35) especially of things named when it was still the prevailing spelling.[[28]](#cite_note-WK15-28) (In British English a "theatre" is where stage performances take place but not film screenings – these take place in a cinema,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] or "picture theatre" in Australia.)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

It is sometimes claimed that, in the US, these two spellings have different meanings, with *theatre* referring to dramatic arts and *theater* referring to buildings.[[28]](#cite_note-WK15-28) However, the trade magazine *[American Theatre](/wiki/American_Theatre_(magazine) "American Theatre (magazine)")* described this as a "popular myth" writing, "not only is there no etymological basis for this rather arbitrary distinction, it’s also not borne out empirically in usage."[[28]](#cite_note-WK15-28)

Some placenames in the United States use *[Centre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/centre "wikt:centre")* in their names. Examples include the villages of [Newton Centre](/wiki/Newton_Centre "Newton Centre") and [Rockville Centre](/wiki/Rockville_Centre,_New_York "Rockville Centre, New York"), the city of [Centreville](/wiki/Centreville,_Illinois "Centreville, Illinois"), [Centre County](/wiki/Centre_County,_Pennsylvania "Centre County, Pennsylvania") and [Centre College](/wiki/Centre_College "Centre College"). Sometimes, these places were named before spelling changes but more often the spelling serves as an affectation. [Proper names](/wiki/Proper_name "Proper name") are usually spelled according to their native-variety spelling vocabulary; so, for instance, although *Peter* is the usual form of the male given name, as a surname both the spellings *Peter* and *Petre* (the latter notably borne by a British [lord](/wiki/Baron_Petre "Baron Petre")) are found.

For British *accoutre*, the American practice varies: the *[Merriam-Webster Dictionary](/wiki/Merriam-Webster_Dictionary "Merriam-Webster Dictionary")* prefers the *-re* spelling,[[37]](#cite_note-37) but *[The American Heritage Dictionary of the English Language](/wiki/The_American_Heritage_Dictionary_of_the_English_Language "The American Heritage Dictionary of the English Language")* prefers the *-er* spelling.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

More recent French loanwords keep the *-re* spelling in American English. These are not exceptions when a French-style pronunciation is used (/rə/ rather than /ə(r)/), as with *[double entendre](/wiki/Double_entendre "Double entendre")*, *[genre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/genre "wikt:genre")* and *[oeuvre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/oeuvre "wikt:oeuvre")*. The unstressed /ə(r)/ pronunciation of an *-er* ending is used either as the most common variant or an alternative pronunciation with some words, including *[cadre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/cadre "wikt:cadre")*, *[macabre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/macabre "wikt:macabre")*, *[maître d'](/wiki/Ma%C3%AEtre_d%27 "Maître d'")*, [Notre Dame](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Notre_Dame "wikt:Notre Dame"), *[piastre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/piastre "wikt:piastre")*, and *[timbre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/timbre "wikt:timbre")*.

#### Commonwealth usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Commonwealth usage")]

The *-re* endings are mostly standard throughout the Commonwealth. The *-er* spellings are recognized as minor variants in Canada, partly due to United States influence. They are sometimes used in proper names (such as Toronto's controversially named [Centerpoint Mall](/wiki/Centerpoint_Mall_(Toronto) "Centerpoint Mall (Toronto)")).[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12)

### *-ce*, *-se*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: -ce, -se")]

For *[advice](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/advice "wikt:advice")*/*[advise](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/advise "wikt:advise")* and *[device](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/device "wikt:device")*/*[devise](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/devise "wikt:devise")*, American English and British English both keep the noun–verb distinction both graphically and phonetically (where the pronunciation is -/s/ for the noun and -/z/ for the verb). For *[licence](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/licence "wikt:licence")/[license](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/license "wikt:license")* or *[practice](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/practice "wikt:practice")/[practise](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/practise "wikt:practise")*, British English also keeps the noun–verb distinction graphically (although phonetically the two words in each pair are [homophones](/wiki/Homophone "Homophone") with -/s/ pronunciation). On the other hand, American English uses *[license](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/license "wikt:license")* and *[practice](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/practice "wikt:practice")* for both nouns and verbs (with -/s/ pronunciation in both cases too).

American English has kept the Anglo-French spelling for *[defense](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/defense "wikt:defense")* and *[offense](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/offense "wikt:offense")*, which are *[defence](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/defence "wikt:defence")* and *[offence](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/offence "wikt:offence")* in British English. Likewise, there are the American *[pretense](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pretense "wikt:pretense")* and British *[pretence](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pretence "wikt:pretence")*; but derivatives such as *[defensive](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/defensive "wikt:defensive")*, *[offensive](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/offensive "wikt:offensive")*, and *[pretension](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pretension "wikt:pretension")* are always thus spelled in both systems.

Australian[[39]](#cite_note-39) and Canadian usages generally follow British usage.

### *-xion*, *-ction*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: -xion, -ction")]

The spelling *[connexion](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/connexion "wikt:connexion")* is now rare in everyday British usage, its use lessening as knowledge of Latin attenuates,[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) and it has almost never been used in the US: the more common *connection* has become the standard worldwide. According to the *Oxford English Dictionary*, the older spelling is more etymologically conservative, since the original Latin word had *-xio-*. The American usage comes from [Webster](/wiki/Noah_Webster "Noah Webster"), who abandoned *-xion* and preferred *-ction*.[[40]](#cite_note-40) *Connexion* was still the house style of *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* of London until the 1980s and was still used by [Post Office Telecommunications](/wiki/Post_Office_Telecommunications "Post Office Telecommunications") for its telephone services in the 1970s, but had by then been overtaken by *connection* in regular usage (for example, in more popular newspapers). *Connexion* (and its derivatives *connexional* and *connexionalism*) is still in use by the [Methodist Church of Great Britain](/wiki/Methodist_Church_of_Great_Britain "Methodist Church of Great Britain") to refer to the whole church as opposed to its constituent districts, circuits and local churches, whereas the US-based [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") uses *Connection*.

*Complexion* (from *complex*) is the standard form worldwide, though *complection* does exist as an uncommon variant.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) The adjective *complected* (as in "dark-complected"), although sometimes proscribed, is on equal footing in the United States with *complexioned.*[[43]](#cite_note-43) It is not used in this way in the UK, although there exists a rare alternative meaning of *complicated*.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Greek-derived and Latin-derived spellings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Greek-derived and Latin-derived spellings")]

### *ae* and *oe*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: ae and oe")]

See also: [English orthography § Ligatures](/wiki/English_orthography#Ligatures "English orthography")

Many words, especially medical words, that are written with *ae/æ* or *oe/œ* in British English are written with just an *e* in American English.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The sounds in question are /iː/ or /ɛ/ (or, unstressed, /i/, /ɪ/ or /ə/). Examples (with non-American letter in **bold**): *[**a**eon](/wiki/Aeon "Aeon")*, *[an**a**emia](/wiki/Anemia "Anemia")*, *[an**a**esthesia](/wiki/Anesthesia "Anesthesia")*, *[c**a**ecum](/wiki/Caecum "Caecum")*, *[c**a**esium](/wiki/Caesium "Caesium")*, *[c**o**eliac](/wiki/Celiac_(disambiguation) "Celiac (disambiguation)")*, *[diarrh**o**ea](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea")*, *[encyclop**a**edia](/wiki/Encyclopedia "Encyclopedia")*, *[f**a**eces](/wiki/Feces "Feces")*, *[f**o**etal](/wiki/Fetal "Fetal")*, *[gyn**a**ecology](/wiki/Gynecology "Gynecology")*, *[h**a**emoglobin](/wiki/Haemoglobin "Haemoglobin")*, *[h**a**emophilia](/wiki/Hemophilia "Hemophilia")*, *[leuk**a**emia](/wiki/Leukemia "Leukemia")*, *[**o**esophagus](/wiki/Esophagus "Esophagus")*, *[**o**estrogen](/wiki/Estrogen "Estrogen")*, *[orthop**a**edic](/wiki/Orthopedic "Orthopedic")*,[[note 1]](#cite_note-orthopaedic-46) *[pal**a**eontology](/wiki/Paleontology "Paleontology")*, *[p**a**ediatric](/wiki/Pediatric "Pediatric")*, *[p**a**edophile](/wiki/Pedophile "Pedophile")*.

Words that can be spelled either way in American English include ***a**esthetics*, *am**o**eba* and *arch**a**eology* (which usually prevail over *esthetics*, *ameba* and *archeology*),[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) as well as *p**a**ean*[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48), *pal**a**estra*[[48]](#cite_note-49), *ph**o**enix*[[49]](#cite_note-50), *subp**o**ena*[[50]](#cite_note-51). *[Oenology](/wiki/Oenology "Oenology")* is acceptable in American English but is deemed a minor variant of *[enology](/wiki/Enology "Enology")*. The chemical *[haem](/wiki/Heme "Heme")* (named as a shortening of *[h**a**emoglobin](/wiki/Hemoglobin "Hemoglobin")*) is spelled *heme* in American English, to avoid confusion with *hem*.

Words that can be spelled either way in British English include *cham**a**eleon*, *encyclop**a**edia*, *hom**o**eopathy*, *medi**a**eval* (a minor variant in both AmE and BrE[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54)), *f**o**etid* and *f**o**etus*. The spellings *f**o**etus* and *f**o**etal* are Britishisms based on a mistaken etymology.[[54]](#cite_note-BMJ-55) The etymologically correct original spelling *fetus* reflects the Latin original and is the standard spelling in medical journals worldwide;[[55]](#cite_note-56) the Oxford English Dictionary notes that "In Latin manuscripts both *fētus* and *foetus* are used".[[56]](#cite_note-57)

The [Ancient Greek](/wiki/History_of_Greek#Ancient_Greek_dialects "History of Greek") [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong") <αι> and <οι> were [transliterated](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") into Latin as <ae> and <oe>. The [ligatures](/wiki/Typographic_ligature "Typographic ligature") [æ](/wiki/%C3%86 "Æ") and [œ](/wiki/%C5%92 "Œ") were introduced when the sounds became [monophthongs](/wiki/Monophthong "Monophthong"), and were later applied to words not of Greek origin in both Latin (for example, *cœli*)) and French (for example, *œuvre*). In English, which has adopted words from all three languages, it is now usual to replace *Æ/æ* with *Ae/ae* and *Œ/œ* with *Oe/oe*. In many words, the digraph has been reduced to a lone *e* in all varieties of English: for example, ***o**economics*, *pr**a**emium*, and ***a**enigma*.[[57]](#cite_note-58) In other cases, the digraph is retained across all varieties. Despite *[phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(mythology) "Phoenix (mythology)")* and *subpoena* being the most common spellings in American English, the uncommon variants *phenix*[[58]](#cite_note-59) and *subpena*[[59]](#cite_note-60) are occasionally used, with *[Phenix](/wiki/Phenix,_Virginia "Phenix, Virginia")* in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") illustrating the variant spelling. The retention of the digraph is especially common in names: *[Aegean](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea")* (the sea), *[Caesar](/wiki/Caesar_(disambiguation) "Caesar (disambiguation)")*, *[Oedipus](/wiki/Oedipus "Oedipus")*, *Phoebe*, etc., although "caesarean section" may be spelled as "cesarean section". There is no reduction of [Latin -ae plurals](/wiki/Latin_declension#First_declension_(a) "Latin declension") (e.g., *larv**ae***); nor where the digraph <ae>/<oe> does not result from the Greek-style ligature as, for example, in *[maelstrom](/wiki/Whirlpool "Whirlpool")* or *toe*; the same is true for the British form *[aeroplane](/wiki/Aeroplane "Aeroplane")* (compare other *aero-* words such as *[aerosol](/wiki/Aerosol "Aerosol")*). The now chiefly North American *[airplane](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/airplane "wikt:airplane")* is not a respelling but a recoining, modelled after *[airship](/wiki/Airship "Airship")* and *aircraft*. The word *airplane* dates from 1907,[[60]](#cite_note-61) at which time the prefix *aero-* was trisyllabic, often written *aëro-*.

Canadian English mostly follows American English in this respect. While *e* is generally preferred over *oe* and often over *ae*,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] *oe* and *ae* are sometimes found in academic, scientific and official publications (for example, the fee schedule of the [Ontario Health Insurance Plan](/wiki/Ontario_Health_Insurance_Plan "Ontario Health Insurance Plan")), and in words such as *palaeontology* or *aeon*.[[61]](#cite_note-Macquarie_Dictionary_8th_Edition-62)

*Pediatrician* is preferred roughly 10 to 1 over *paediatrician*, while *foetal* and *oestrogen* are similarly uncommon. Canadian English is split on *gynecology* (e.g., the [Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists of Canada](/wiki/Society_of_Obstetricians_and_Gynaecologists_of_Canada "Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists of Canada") vs. the [Canadian Medical Association](/wiki/Canadian_Medical_Association "Canadian Medical Association")'s Canadian specialty profile of *Obstetrics/gynecology*). While British and American English both use the spelling *"pal**ae**olithic"*, the standard Canadian English spelling is usually *"pal**e**olithic"*, which is one of the very few instances where the Canadian spelling of a word differs from both the British and American spellings of that word.[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)

In Australia, it can go either way, depending on the word: *medieval*, *eon* and *fetus* follow the American usage with *e* rather than *ae* or *oe*; *oestrogen* and *paediatrician* are spelled the British way.[[61]](#cite_note-Macquarie_Dictionary_8th_Edition-62) *Manoeuvre* is the only spelling in Australia, and the most common one in Canada, where *maneuver* and *manoeuver* are also sometimes found.[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) Elsewhere, the British usage prevails, but the spellings with just *e* are increasingly used worldwide. With the exception of *manoeuvre*, most British and American spellings are usually considered acceptable variants.[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12)[[8]](#cite_note-Fourth_Edition_2005-8)

## Greek-derived spellings (often through Latin and Romance)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Greek-derived spellings (often through Latin and Romance)")]

### *-ise*, *-ize* (*-isation*, *-ization*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: -ise, -ize (-isation, -ization)")]

See also: [Oxford spelling](/wiki/Oxford_spelling "Oxford spelling")

#### Origin and recommendations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Origin and recommendations")]

The *[-ize](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-ize "wikt:-ize")* spelling is often incorrectly seen in the United Kingdom as an Americanism. It has been in use since the 15th century, predating the *[-ise](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-ise "wikt:-ise")* spelling by over a century.[[64]](#cite_note-IZE-65) The verb-forming suffix *-ize* comes directly from Ancient Greek [-ίζειν](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-%CE%AF%CE%B6%CE%B5%CE%B9%CE%BD "wikt:-ίζειν") (*[-ízein](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-%C3%ADzein "wikt:-ízein")*) or Late Latin *[-izāre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-izare "wikt:-izare")*, while *-ise* comes via French *[-iser](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-iser "wikt:-iser")*, which itself stems from the same Greek suffix.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-:1-67) The *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* (*OED*) recommends *-ize* and lists the *-ise* form as an alternative.[[66]](#cite_note-:1-67)

Publications by [Oxford University Press](/wiki/Oxford_University_Press "Oxford University Press") (OUP)—such as [Henry Watson Fowler](/wiki/Henry_Watson_Fowler "Henry Watson Fowler")'s *[A Dictionary of Modern English Usage](/wiki/A_Dictionary_of_Modern_English_Usage "A Dictionary of Modern English Usage")*, *[Hart's Rules](/wiki/Hart%27s_Rules "Hart's Rules")*,[[67]](#cite_note-hhart-68) and *The Oxford Guide to English Usage*[[68]](#cite_note-69)—also recommend *-ize*. However, Robert Allan's *Pocket Fowler's Modern English Usage* considers either spelling to be acceptable anywhere but the US.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

#### Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Usage")]

American spelling avoids *-ise* endings in words like *organize*, *[realize](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/realize "wikt:realize")* and *recognize*.[[70]](#cite_note-AOZ-71)

British spelling mostly uses *-ise* (*organise*, *[realise](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/realise "wikt:realise")*, *recognise*), though *-ize* is sometimes used.[[70]](#cite_note-AOZ-71) The ratio between *-ise* and *-ize* stood at 3:2 in the [British National Corpus](/wiki/British_National_Corpus "British National Corpus") up to 2002.[[71]](#cite_note-72) The spelling *-ise* is more commonly used in UK mass media and newspapers,[[70]](#cite_note-AOZ-71) including *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* (which switched conventions in 1992),[[72]](#cite_note-Dixon2004-73) *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*, *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* and the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"). The [Government of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") additionally uses *-ise*, stating "do not use Americanisms" justifying that the spelling "is often seen as such".[[73]](#cite_note-74) The *-ize* form is known as [Oxford spelling](/wiki/Oxford_spelling "Oxford spelling") and is used in publications of the Oxford University Press, most notably the *Oxford English Dictionary*, and of other academic publishers[[74]](#cite_note-75) such as *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")*, the *[Biochemical Journal](/wiki/Biochemical_Journal "Biochemical Journal")* and *[The Times Literary Supplement](/wiki/The_Times_Literary_Supplement "The Times Literary Supplement")*. It can be identified using the [IETF language tag](/wiki/IETF_language_tag "IETF language tag") **en-GB-oxendict** (or, historically, by **en-GB-oed**).[[75]](#cite_note-76)

In Ireland, India, Australia, and New Zealand[[76]](#cite_note-77) *-ise* spellings strongly prevail: the *-ise* form is preferred in Australian English at a ratio of about 3:1 according to the *[Macquarie Dictionary](/wiki/Macquarie_Dictionary "Macquarie Dictionary")*.

In Canada, the *-ize* ending is more common, although the *Ontario Public School Spelling Book*[[77]](#cite_note-78) spelled most words in the *-ize* form, but allowed for duality with a page insert as late as the 1970s, noting that, although the *-ize* spelling was in fact the convention used in the [*OED*](/wiki/Oxford_spelling#Defining_feature "Oxford spelling"), the choice to spell such words in the *-ise* form was *a matter of personal preference*; however, a *pupil* having made the decision, one way or the other, thereafter ought to write uniformly not only for a given word, but to apply that same uniformity consistently for all words where the option is found. Just as with [*-yze*](#-yse,_-yze) spellings, however, in Canada the *ize* form remains the preferred or more common spelling, though both can still be found, yet the *-ise* variation, once more common amongst older Canadians, is employed less and less often in favour of the *-ize* spelling. (The alternate convention offered as a matter of choice may have been due to the fact that although there were an increasing number of American- and British-based dictionaries with Canadian Editions by the late 1970s, these were largely only supplemental in terms of vocabulary with subsequent definitions. It was not until the mid-1990s[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80) that Canadian-based dictionaries became increasingly common.)

Worldwide, *-ize* endings prevail in scientific writing and are commonly used by many international organizations, such as [United Nations Organizations](/wiki/United_Nations_Organization "United Nations Organization") (such as the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") and the [International Civil Aviation Organization](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization")) and the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") (but not by the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development")). The [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union")'s style guides require the usage of -*ise*.[[80]](#cite_note-EUStyleGuide-81) Proofreaders at the EU's Publications Office ensure consistent spelling in official publications such as the *[Official Journal of the European Union](/wiki/Official_Journal_of_the_European_Union "Official Journal of the European Union")* (where legislation and other official documents are published), but the *-ize* spelling may be found in other documents.

The same applies to [inflections](/wiki/Inflections "Inflections") and derivations such as *[colonised](/wiki/Colonised "Colonised")*/*[colonized](/wiki/Colonized "Colonized")* and *modernisation*/*modernization*.

#### Exceptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Exceptions")]

* Some verbs take only an *-ize* form worldwide. In these, *-ize* is not a suffix, so does not ultimately come from Ancient Greek -ίζειν: for example, *capsize*, *seize* (except in the legal phrases *to be [seised](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/seised "wikt:seised") of* or *to stand seised to*), *size* and *prize* (meaning *value*, as opposed to the *prise* that means *pry*).
* Some verbs take only *-s-* worldwide. In these, *-ise* is not a suffix, but part of the English, French or Latin stems *-rise, -vise, -mis-*, etc.: *advise*, *arise*, *chastise*, *circumcise*, *compromise*, *demise*, *despise*, *devise*, *disguise*, *excise*, *exercise*, *franchise*, *guise*, *improvise*, *incise*, *promise*, *reprise*, *revise*, *rise*, *surmise*, *televise*, and *wise*. Less common *-ize* variants such as *advertize*[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83), *comprize*[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-85), and *surprize*[[85]](#cite_note-86) do exist in American English, but are rarer than the *-ise* forms.
* Some words spelled with *-ize* in American English are [not used in British English](/wiki/List_of_American_words_not_widely_used_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of American words not widely used in the United Kingdom"). For example, from the noun *[burglar](/wiki/Burglar "Burglar")*, the usual verb is formed by suffixation in American English (*burglarize*) but [back-formation](/wiki/Back-formation "Back-formation") in British English (*burgle*).[[86]](#cite_note-87)
* Conversely, the verb *to prise* (meaning "to force" or "to lever") is rarely used in North American English:[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) *pry* is instead used, a back-formation from or alteration of *prise* to avoid confusion with the more common noun "prize". When it is used in Canada, it is spelled with an *s*, just as it is in British, Irish, Indian, New Zealand and European English, where its use is more common. However, the rare occurrences in the US have the spelling as *prize* even though it does not contain a suffix, so does not derive from -ίζειν.[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89)
* The spelling *enterprize* was the dominant spelling throughout the 18th century[[89]](#cite_note-90); Royal Navy vessels such as [HMS Enterprize (1743)](/wiki/HMS_Enterprize_(1743) "HMS Enterprize (1743)") also used this spelling. However, *enterprize* is now considered archaic and is no longer used in either British or American English.

### *-yse*, *-yze*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: -yse, -yze")]

The ending *-yse* is now British and *-yze* is American. Thus, in British English *[analyse](/wiki/Analyse "Analyse")*, *[catalyse](/wiki/Catalyse "Catalyse")*, *[hydrolyse](/wiki/Hydrolyse "Hydrolyse")* and *[paralyse](/wiki/Paralyse "Paralyse")*, but in American English *[analyze](/wiki/Analyze "Analyze")*, *[catalyze](/wiki/Catalyze "Catalyze")*, *[hydrolyze](/wiki/Hydrolyze "Hydrolyze")* and *[paralyze](/wiki/Paralyze "Paralyze")*.

Either ending is derived from the Greek noun stem *[λύσις](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BB%CF%8D%CF%83%CE%B9%CF%82 "wikt:λύσις")* *[lysis](/wiki/Lysis "Lysis")* ("release") with the *-ize/-ise* suffix added to it, and not the original verb form, whose stem is *λυ- ly-* without the *-s/z-* segment. The Oxford English Dictionary states on that matter: [[90]](#cite_note-91)

> On Greek analogies the verb would have been *analysize,* French *analysiser,* of which [French] *analyser* was practically a shortened form, since, though following the analogy of pairs like *annexe, annexe-r,* it rested chiefly on the fact that by form-association it appeared already to belong to the series of factitive verbs in French -*iser,* English *-ize*, = Latin -*īzāre,* from Greek -ίζ-ειν, to which in sense it belonged. Hence from the first it was commonly written in Eng. *analyze,* the spelling accepted by Johnson, and historically quite defensible.

Alongside the authoritative [Samuel Johnson’s Dictionary, 1755](/wiki/A_Dictionary_of_the_English_Language "A Dictionary of the English Language"), the spelling *analyze* was also preferred by [John Kersey's of 1702](/wiki/Dictionarium_Anglo-Britannicum "Dictionarium Anglo-Britannicum") and [Nathan Bailey's of 1721](/wiki/An_Universal_Etymological_English_Dictionary "An Universal Etymological English Dictionary"), both published in London. It is also given (alongside with *analyse*) as one of the two equally significant "main forms" in the first (published 1884-1928) and second (published 1989) editions of the Oxford English Dictionary.

In Canada, *-yze* is now generally preferred[[91]](#cite_note-92), but *-yse* is also very common. In South Africa, Australia and New Zealand, *-yse* is the prevailing form.

### *-ogue*, *-og*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: -ogue, -og")]

British and other Commonwealth English use the ending *-logue* while American English commonly uses the ending *-log* for words like *analog(ue)*, *catalog(ue)*, *homolog(ue)*, etc., etymologically derived from Greek [-λόγος](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-%CE%BB%CF%8C%CE%B3%CE%BF%CF%82 "wikt:-λόγος") *-logos* ("one who speaks (in a certain manner)"). The *-gue* spelling, as in *catalogue*, is used in the US, but *catalog* is more common. In contrast, *dialogue*, *epilogue*, *prologue*, and *monologue* are extremely common spellings compared to *dialog* etc. in American English, although both forms are treated as acceptable ways to spell the words[[92]](#cite_note-93) (thus, the inflected forms, *cataloged* and *cataloging* vs. *catalogued* and *cataloguing*).

In American English, *analog* is the standard spelling for the adjective (e.g., *analog signal*), while *analogue* is sometimes preferred for the noun. According to *Merriam-Webster's Collegiate Dictionary*, *analog* is listed as the primary adjective form, and *analogue* as the principal noun form, with *analog* being labeled as a variant.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

In Australia, *analog* is standard for the adjective,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] but both *analogue* and *analog* are current for the noun; in all other cases the *-gue* endings strongly prevail,[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) for example *monologue*, except for such expressions as *[dialog box](/wiki/Dialog_box "Dialog box")* in computing,[[94]](#cite_note-95) which are also used in other Commonwealth countries. In Australia, *analog* is used in its technical and electronic sense, as in *analog electronics*.[[8]](#cite_note-Fourth_Edition_2005-8) In Canada and New Zealand, *analogue* is used, but *analog* has some currency as a technical term[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) (e.g., in electronics, as in "analog electronics" as opposed to "digital electronics" and some video-game consoles might have an *analog stick*). The *-ue* is absent worldwide in related words like *analogy*, *analogous*, and *analogist*.

Words such as *demagogue*, *pedagogue*, and *synagogue*, from the Greek noun ἀγωγός *agōgos* ("guide"), are more commonly spelled with *‑ue* in American English, though the shorter forms *demagog*,[[95]](#cite_note-96) *pedagog*,[[96]](#cite_note-97) and *synagog*[[97]](#cite_note-98) also exist and are accepted variants.

Both British and American English use the spelling *-gue* with a silent *-ue* for certain words that are not part of the *-ogue* set, such as *tongue*, *plague*, *vague*, and *league.* In addition, when the *-ue* is not silent, as in the words *argue,* *ague* and *segue,* all varieties of English use *-gue.*

## Doubled consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Doubled consonants")]

The plural of the noun *bus* is usually *buses*, with *busses* a minor American variant.[[98]](#cite_note-bus_cald-99) Conversely, inflections of the verb *bus* usually double the *s* in British usage (*busses, bussed, bussing*) but not American usage (*buses, bused, busing*).[[98]](#cite_note-bus_cald-99) In Australia, both are common, with the American usage slightly more common.[[99]](#cite_note-100)

### Doubled in British English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Doubled in British English")]

The final consonant of an English word is sometimes doubled in both American and British spelling when adding a suffix beginning with a vowel, for example *strip/stripped*, which prevents confusion with *stripe/striped* and shows the difference in pronunciation (see [digraph](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography)#Double_letters "Digraph (orthography)")). Generally, this happens only when the word's final syllable is stressed and when it also ends with a lone vowel followed by a lone consonant. In British English, however, a final *-l* is often doubled even when the final syllable is unstressed.[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) This exception is no longer usual in American English, seemingly because of [Noah Webster](/wiki/Noah_Webster "Noah Webster").[[100]](#cite_note-101) The *-ll-* spellings are nevertheless still deemed acceptable variants by both Merriam-Webster Collegiate and American Heritage dictionaries.

* The British English doubling is used for all inflections (*-ed*, *-ing*, *-er*, *-est*) and for the noun suffixes *-er* and *-or*. Therefore, British English usage is *cancelled*, *counsellor*, *cruellest*, *labelled*, *modelling*, *quarrelled*, *signalling*, *traveller*, and *travelling*. Americans typically use *canceled*, *counselor*, *cruelest*, *labeled*, *modeling*, *quarreled*, *signaling*, *traveler*, and *traveling*. However, for certain words such as *cancelled*, the *-ll-* spelling is acceptable in American English as well.
  + The word *parallel* keeps a single *-l-* in British English, as in American English (*paralleling*, *unparalleled*), to avoid the cluster *-llell-*.
  + Words with two vowels before a final *l* are also spelled with *-ll-* in British English before a suffix when the first vowel either acts as a consonant (*equalling* and *initialled*; in the United States, *equaling* or *initialed*), or belongs to a separate syllable (British *di•alled* and *fu•el•ling*; American *di•aled* and *fue•ling*).
    - British *woollen* is a further exception due to the double vowel (American: *woolen*). Also, *wooly* is accepted in American English, though *woolly* prevails in both systems.[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12)
    - The verb *surveil*, a [back-formation](/wiki/Back-formation "Back-formation") from *surveillance*, always makes *surveilled*, *surveilling*.[[101]](#cite_note-102)
* Endings *-ize*/*-ise*, *-ism*, *-ist*, *-ish* usually do not double the *l* in British English; for example, *devilish*, *dualism*, *normalise*, and *novelist*.
  + Exceptions: *duellist*, *medallist*, *panellist*, *tranquillise*, and sometimes *triallist* in British English.
* For *-ous*, British English has a single *l* in *scandalous* and *perilous*, but the "ll" in *libellous* and *marvellous*.
* For *-ee*, British English has *libellee*.
* For *-age*, British English has *[pupillage](/wiki/Pupillage "Pupillage")* but *vassalage*.
* American English sometimes has an unstressed *-ll-*, as in the UK, in some words where the root has *-l*. These are cases where the change happens in the source language, which was often Latin. (Examples: *bimetallism*, *cancellation*, *chancellor*, *crystallize*, *excellent*, *raillery*, and *tonsillitis*.)
* All forms of English have *compelled*, *excelling*, *propelled*, *rebelling* (stressed *-ll-*); *revealing*, *fooling* (double vowel before the *l*); and *hurling* (consonant before the *l*).
* Canadian and Australian English mostly follow British usage.[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12)

Among consonants other than *l*, practice varies for some words, such as where the final syllable has [secondary stress](/wiki/Secondary_stress "Secondary stress") or an unreduced vowel. In the United States, the spellings *kidnaped* and *worshiped*, which were introduced by the *[Chicago Tribune](/wiki/Chicago_Tribune "Chicago Tribune")* in the 1920s,[[102]](#cite_note-103) are common, but *kidnapped* and *worshipped* prevail.[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105) *Kidnapped* and *worshipped* are the only standard British spellings. However, *focused* is the predominant spelling in both British and American English, *focussed* being just a minor variant in British English.[[105]](#cite_note-106)

Miscellaneous:

* British *calliper* or *caliper*; American *caliper*.
* British *jewellery*; American *jewelry*. The word originates from the Old French word *jouel*[[106]](#cite_note-107) (whose contemporary French equivalent is *joyau*, with the same meaning). The standard pronunciation [/ˈdʒuːəlri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")[[107]](#cite_note-108) does not reflect this difference, but the non-standard pronunciation [/ˈdʒuːləri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") (which exists in New Zealand and the United Kingdom, hence the Cockney rhyming slang word *tomfoolery* [/tɒmˈfuːləri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) does. According to Fowler, *jewelry* used to be the "rhetorical and poetic" spelling in the UK, and was still used by *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* into the mid-20th century. Canada has both, but *jewellery* is more often used. Likewise, the Commonwealth (including Canada) has *jeweller* and the US has *jeweler* for a jewel(le)ry seller.

### Doubled in American English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Doubled in American English")]

Conversely, there are words where British writers prefer a single *l* and Americans a double *l*. In American usage, the spelling of words is usually not changed when they form the main part (not prefix or suffix) of other words, especially in newly formed words and in words whose main part is in common use. Words with this spelling difference include *appall*, *enrollment*, *fulfillment*, *installment*, *skillful*, *thralldom*, *willful*. These words have monosyllabic cognates always written with *-ll*: *pall* (verb), *roll*, *fill*, *stall*, *skill*, *thrall*, *will*. Cases where a single *l* nevertheless occurs in both American and British English include *null*→*annul*, *annulment*; *till*→*until* (although some prefer *til* to reflect the single *l* in *until*, sometimes using a leading apostrophe (*'til*); this should be considered a [hypercorrection](/wiki/Hypercorrection "Hypercorrection") as *till* predates the use of *until*); and others where the connection is not clear or the monosyllabic cognate is not in common use in American English (e.g., *null* is used mainly as a technical term in law, mathematics, and computer science).

In the UK, a single *l* is generally preferred over American forms *distill*, *enroll*, *enthrall*, and *instill*, although *ll* was formerly used;[[108]](#cite_note-109) these are always spelled with *ll* in American usage. The former British spellings *dulness*, *fulness*, and *instal* are now quite rare.[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) The Scottish *[tolbooth](/wiki/Tolbooth "Tolbooth")* is cognate with *[tollbooth](/wiki/Tollbooth_(disambiguation) "Tollbooth (disambiguation)")*, but it has a distinct meaning.

In both American and British usages, words normally spelled *-ll* usually drop the second *l* when used as prefixes or suffixes, for example *all*→*almighty*, *altogether*; *full*→*handful*, *useful*; *well*→*welcome*, *welfare*; *chill*→*chilblain*.

Both the British *fulfil* and the American *fulfill* never use *-ll-* in the middle (i.e., \**fullfill* and \**fullfil* are incorrect).[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-111)

Johnson wavered on this issue. His dictionary of 1755 [lemmatizes](/wiki/Lemmatization "Lemmatization") *distil* and *instill*, *downhil* and *uphill*.[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12)

## Dropped "e"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Dropped \"e\"")]

British English sometimes keeps a silent "e" when adding suffixes where American English does not. Generally speaking, British English drops it in only some cases in which it is needed to show pronunciation whereas American English only uses it where needed.

* British prefers *ageing*,[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) American usually *aging* (compare *ageism*, *raging*). For the noun or verb "route", British English often uses *routeing*,[[111]](#cite_note-112) but in the US, *routing* is used. The military term *rout* forms *routing* everywhere. However, all of these words form "router", whether used in the context of carpentry, data communications, or the military. (e.g., "Attacus was the router of the Huns at ....")

Both forms of English keep the silent "e" in the words *dyeing*, *singeing*, and *swingeing*[[112]](#cite_note-113) (in the sense of *dye*, *singe*, and *swinge*), to distinguish them from *dying*, *singing*, *swinging* (in the sense of *die*, *sing*, and *swing*). In contrast, the verb *bathe* and the British verb *bath* both form *bathing*. Both forms of English vary for *tinge* and *twinge*; both prefer *cringing*, *hinging*, *lunging*, *syringing*.

* Before *-able*, British English prefers *likeable*, *liveable*, *rateable*, *saleable*, *sizeable*, *unshakeable*,[[113]](#cite_note-British_National_Corpus-114) where American practice prefers to drop the "-e"; but both British and American English prefer *breathable*, *curable*, *datable*, *lovable*, *movable*, *notable*, *provable*, *quotable*, *scalable*, *solvable*, *usable*,[[113]](#cite_note-British_National_Corpus-114) and those where the root is polysyllabic, like *believable* or *decidable*. Both systems keep the silent "e" when it is needed to preserve a soft "c", "ch", or "g", such as in *cacheable*, *changeable*, *traceable*; both usually keep the "e" after "-dge", as in *knowledgeable*, *unbridgeable*, and *unabridgeable* ("These rights are unabridgeable").
* Both *abridgment* and the more regular *abridgement* are current in the US, only the latter in the UK.[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) Likewise for the word *lodg(e)ment*. Both *judgment* and *judgement* are in use interchangeably everywhere, although the former prevails in the US and the latter prevails in the UK[[12]](#cite_note-Peters-12) except in the practice of law, where *judgment* is standard. This also holds for *abridgment* and *acknowledgment*. Both systems prefer *[fledgling](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/fledgling "wikt:fledgling")* to *fledgeling*, but *[ridgeling](/wiki/Ridgeling "Ridgeling")* to *ridgling*. *Acknowledgment*, *acknowledgement*, *abridgment* and *abridgement* are all used in Australia; the shorter forms are endorsed by the Australian Capital Territory Government.[[8]](#cite_note-Fourth_Edition_2005-8)[[114]](#cite_note-115) Apart from when the "e" is dropped and in the words *mortgagor* and *[gaol](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/gaol "wikt:gaol")* and some pronunciations of *margarine*, "g" can only be soft when followed by an "e", "i", or "y".
* The word "blue" always drops the "e" when forming "bluish" or "bluing".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Past tense differences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_and_British_English_spelling_differences&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Past tense differences")]

In the UK, Ireland, Australia, New Zealand and Canada, it is more common to end some past tense verbs with a "t" as in *[learnt](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/learnt "wikt:learnt")* or *[dreamt](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/dreamt "wikt:dreamt")* rather than *learned* or *dreamed,*[[155]](#cite_note-156) though such spellings are also found in American English. However, in American English, *burned* and *burnt* have different usages.

Several verbs have different past tenses or past participles in American and British English:

* The past tense of the verb "to dive" is most commonly found as "dived" in British and New Zealand English. "Dove" is sometimes used in its place in American English.[[156]](#cite_note-157) Both terms are understood in Canada and Australia, and may be found either in minority use or in regional dialect in the US.
* The past tense of the verb "to get" is "got" everywhere, but the past participle is "got" in British and New Zealand English but "gotten" in American and Canadian, and occasionally in Australian English. Both terms are understood, and may be found either in minority use or in regional dialect. One exception is in the phrase "ill-gotten", which is widely used everywhere. Another is the universal use of "have got" to indicate possession or necessity: "I have got a car", "I have got to go" (whereas "I have gotten a car" would mean "I have *obtained* a car", and "I have gotten to go" would mean "I have had the *opportunity/privilege* to go"). None of this affects "forget" and "beget", whose past participles are "forgotten" and "begotten" in all varieties.
